perturbation.original_text: Any dispute or controversy arising out of or relating to any interpretation, construction, performance, or breach of this Agreement shall be resolved exclusively by binding arbitration in Las Vegas, Nevada, in accordance with the rules then in effect of the American Arbitration Association, The arbitrator(s) may grant injunctions or other relief in such dispute or controversy. The decision of the arbitrator(s) shall be final, conclusive, and binding on the parties to the arbitration. Judgment may be entered on the arbitrator's decision in any court having jurisdiction. The parties to the arbitration shall each pay an equal share of the costs and expenses of such <PAGE> arbitration, and each of them shall separately pay their counsel fees and expenses.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute or controversy arising out of or relating to any interpretation, construction, performance, or breach of this Agreement shall be resolved exclusively by binding arbitration in Las Vegas, Nevada. The decision of the arbitrator(s) shall be final, conclusive, and binding on the parties to the arbitration. Judgment may be entered on the arbitrator's decision in any court having jurisdiction. The parties to the arbitration shall each pay an equal share of the costs and expenses of such <PAGE> arbitration, and each of them shall separately pay their counsel fees and expenses.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'in accordance with the rules then in effect of the American Arbitration Association' eliminates the procedural safeguards and consumer protections provided by AAA rules, potentially violating Nevada's arbitration statutes that require fair and reasonable arbitration procedures.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 38.206 mandates that arbitration procedures must be fair to all parties. By removing the requirement to adhere to AAA rules, the contract risks violating this statute by potentially allowing for biased or unfair arbitration proceedings.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the requirement to adhere to AAA rules. The Law Explanation states that Nevada law (NRS 38.206) mandates fair arbitration procedures. By removing the AAA rules, the contract creates a risk of biased arbitration, potentially violating Nevada law. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that the removed clause eliminates consumer protections provided by AAA rules. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not applicable, but the 'Law Explanation' describes the possible violation clearly.

perturbation.original_text: None of the parties shall divulge to any person, (other than those whose provenance it is to know it, or with proper authority) or use any trade secrets or confidential information or any financial or trade information relating to the other, which they acquire as a result of the discussing of or the entering into agreements with each other. Each Party shall endeavor to prevent its officers, employees, agents, representatives, and associates from doing anything, which, if done by Party, would be a breach of this agreement. This restriction shall continue to apply after the expiration of this Agreement, and other Agreements entered into between the Parties, without limit in point of time, but shall cease to apply to secrets or information, which came into the public domain through no fault of the Party concerned.
perturbation.changed_text: None of the parties shall divulge to any person, or use any trade secrets or confidential information or any financial or trade information relating to the other, which they acquire as a result of the discussing of or the entering into agreements with each other. Each Party shall endeavor to prevent its officers, employees, agents, representatives, and associates from doing anything, which, if done by Party, would be a breach of this agreement. This restriction shall continue to apply after the expiration of this Agreement, and other Agreements entered into between the Parties, without limit in point of time, but shall cease to apply to secrets or information, which came into the public domain through no fault of the Party concerned.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception 'other than those whose provenance it is to know it, or with proper authority,' the agreement now overly restricts the sharing of information, potentially conflicting with Nevada's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (NUTSA), which allows for disclosure to those with a 'need to know' within a business context.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 600A.030 defines misappropriation, but also implies the necessity of internal business communications. By omitting the exception clause, the contract could be interpreted as preventing necessary internal disclosures, creating a conflict with the implied scope of the NUTSA.

perturbation.justification: The original contract allowed for the sharing of information to those who 'need to know it, or with proper authority.' The changed text removes this exception, creating an overly broad restriction. The Law Explanation notes this could prevent necessary internal disclosures, creating a conflict with the implied scope of the NUTSA. Even though the scraped snippets are not helpful, the removal of the exception in the contract creates a likely legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties each undertake to the other that for a period of five (5) years from the date of this Agreement, they will not, without prior written consent of the other, directly or indirectly through third parties, make or seek to make contact or communication with those Banks, Financial Institutions, government representatives, clients, investors, traders, associates, legal advisors and financial advisors with whom they have been placed into contact with by the other or to whom the names, addresses and other pertinent information has been released to them by the other.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties each undertake to the other that they will not, directly or indirectly through third parties, make or seek to make contact or communication with those Banks, Financial Institutions, government representatives, clients, investors, traders, associates, legal advisors and financial advisors with whom they have been placed into contact with by the other or to whom the names, addresses and other pertinent information has been released to them by the other.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the time limit of 'five (5) years' creates an indefinite restriction on contacting clients, which could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint of trade under Nevada law, which generally disfavors overly broad non-compete agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada Revised Statutes 613.190 Restraint of trade unlawful; penalty.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 613.190 prohibits combinations to restrain trade. While this isn't directly a non-compete, the removal of the time limit transforms this into a potentially unreasonable restriction on business contacts, which a Nevada court could view as an unlawful restraint, especially if it harms a party's ability to conduct business.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'five (5) years' time limit in the changed text creates an indefinite restriction, potentially conflicting with the law's concern for unreasonable restraints of trade. The Law Explanation mentions NRS 613.190, indicating a prohibition against combinations to restrain trade. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a LexisNexis sign-in page, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest the modified contract might be viewed as an unlawful restraint if it harms a party's ability to conduct business, making the contradiction direct and impactful.
